content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A few years back, I stumbled across two YouTube channels that honestly made me rethink everything I thought I knew about making money online.
One of them was pulling in millions of views a month. Fun videos, loyal fans, comment sections full of energy. The other? Much smaller. Quiet, even. It posted straightforward stuff — personal finance tips, investing basics, software walkthroughs. Most videos barely hit 30,000 views, sometimes 40,000 on a good day.
You’d think the bigger channel was cleaning up financially. I sure did.
Turns out I had it completely backwards.
The smaller channel was making significantly more money. Why? Because it had landed in what’s called a high RPM niche — and that changed everything.
RPM stands for Revenue Per Mille, basically what you earn per 1,000 views after YouTube takes its cut. Entertainment channels might pocket a couple of dollars per thousand views. But creators in certain fields? They can earn several times that, because the advertisers chasing their audience are willing to pay a lot more for that access.
It makes sense when you think about it from the advertiser’s side.
A company selling $1,500 project management software or pushing a premium investment platform can easily justify spending big on ads. Even landing one customer could bring in hundreds — sometimes thousands — of dollars in return. That kind of math drives ad prices up, and creators are the ones who benefit.
So yes, your niche choice matters enormously. More than most beginners realize.
Personal finance
has been a top earner on YouTube for years now. Banks, insurance companies, investment apps — they all want in front of viewers who are searching for budgeting tips, retirement strategies, or credit card comparisons. That competition keeps RPMs high.
Artificial intelligence
is newer to the list but climbing fast. Every business on the planet is trying to figure out how to use AI tools right now. If you’re making videos that explain automation, AI workflows, or practical software use cases, you’re reaching an audience that’s actively looking to spend money on solutions.
Software tutorials
work similarly. Someone Googling “best video editing software” or “how to build a website” isn’t just killing time — they’re probably close to buying something. Advertisers know this, and they pay accordingly.
Digital marketing content
— SEO, email campaigns, Google Ads, affiliate strategies — pulls in business owners and freelancers who already have budgets set aside for tools and services. Same story with business and entrepreneurship channels, where the audience is constantly investing in courses, software, and coaching.
Tech reviews
are worth mentioning too. Laptops, smartphones, productivity tools — these videos attract buyers, not just browsers. And on top of ad revenue, many tech creators stack affiliate income on the side, which makes it one of the more sustainable long-term paths.
